past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 15 years ago, inconsistently controlled
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 10 years ago
Obstructive sleep apnea, diagnosed 4 years ago, non-adherent with CPAP
Chronic kidney disease stage 3a (eGFR 52), attributed to diabetic and hypertensive nephropathy
Remote transient ischemic attack 2 years ago, workup at outside hospital reportedly negative

medications.indication: Secondary stroke prevention after prior TIA

family_history: Mother had a stroke at age 71
Father had type 2 diabetes and died of congestive heart failure at age 74
Sister with hypertension and atrial fibrillation

social_history.smoking: Active smoker, 25 pack-year history, currently smoking half a pack per day
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in a two-story house, bedroom on second floor

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert, oriented to person and place but initially states the year as 2024. Mildly inattentive — makes errors on serial sevens (4 of 5 correct) and months backward (omits September). Speech is mildly dysarthric with slurred consonants but language comprehension and repetition are intact. Can name high-frequency objects but hesitates on low-frequency items. Follows two-step commands bilaterally.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, reactive. Visual fields full to confrontation but extinguishes left visual stimulus on double simultaneous stimulation on the right. No obvious gaze preference at rest, but subtle incomplete rightward gaze on leftward saccades. Mild right lower facial flattening, not clearly a droop. Hearing grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength normal. Tongue midline.
neurological_exam.motor: Right upper extremity: 4+/5 grip strength, 4/5 deltoid, 4+/5 wrist extension, subtle pronator drift on the right when eyes closed. Right lower extremity: 4+/5 hip flexion, 5-/5 knee extension, 4+/5 ankle dorsiflexion. Left upper and lower extremities: 5/5 strength throughout. No fasciculations.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch intact bilaterally. Pinprick mildly diminished in a stocking distribution to mid-calf bilaterally (consistent with known neuropathy). Right-sided extinction on double simultaneous stimulation to light touch. Proprioception intact at great toes bilaterally.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Right biceps 2+, triceps 3+, brachioradialis 2+, patellar 2+, Achilles 1+. Left biceps 2+, triceps 2+, brachioradialis 2+, patellar 2+, Achilles 1+. Right plantar response equivocal (neither clearly upgoing nor downgoing). Left plantar response flexor.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing: right side shows subtle past-pointing on the third attempt, left side intact. Heel-to-shin intact bilaterally. No dysdiadochokinesia.
neurological_exam.gait: Mildly unsteady, tends to drift rightward. Tandem gait impaired with two missteps. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: NIHSS score: 5. Subtle right-sided neglect on line bisection (deviates 1.5 cm to the left of center). No meningismus. No carotid bruits appreciated.

patient.chief_complaint: Woke up with slurred speech and right-sided clumsiness
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Curtis Darnell is a 67-year-old right-handed African American man with a history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes, hyperlipidemia, obstructive sleep apnea, chronic kidney disease stage 3a, and a prior transient ischemic attack who was brought to the emergency department by his wife after she noticed something was wrong when he woke up this morning. His wife reports that he went to bed at approximately 22:30 last night and was entirely at his baseline, having watched the evening news and conversed normally before falling asleep. She awoke at 06:15 this morning and found him already sitting on the edge of the bed. He told her he felt 'off' and she immediately noticed his speech was slurred. When he tried to stand, he stumbled slightly to the right side and had difficulty buttoning his shirt with his right hand. She drove him to the emergency department, arriving at 07:40. The patient himself reports that he woke up around 06:00 and noticed his right hand felt 'clumsy' and his mouth felt 'thick.' He denies any headache, visual changes, nausea, or vomiting. He did not experience any chest pain, palpitations, or shortness of breath. There was no witnessed seizure activity. He denies any recent fever, neck stiffness, or trauma. He admits he has not been taking his blood pressure medications consistently over the past week because he ran out and had not yet refilled the prescriptions. He also acknowledges continued smoking despite medical advice. His wife notes that he has been snoring more heavily than usual over the past several months and has not been using his CPAP device for over a year. She recalls his prior TIA episode two years ago presented similarly with transient right hand clumsiness that resolved within an hour, but this time the symptoms have persisted. Last known well time is established as 22:30 the prior evening, approximately 9 hours before ED arrival, placing him outside the standard IV tPA window.

findings.location: Left posterior frontal and parietal cortex in the left MCA superior division territory, predominantly involving the precentral gyrus and superior parietal lobule
findings.size: Approximately 2.8 x 1.9 cm in maximal axial dimensions

signal_characteristics.DWI: Hyperintense signal consistent with restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.ADC: Corresponding hypointensity confirming true restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense signal in the same distribution, consistent with infarct age greater than 4-6 hours
signal_characteristics.T1: Subtle hypointensity in the affected territory
signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense signal in left frontoparietal region

findings.mass_effect: No significant mass effect. No midline shift.
findings.borders: Margins conform to the left MCA superior division vascular territory

findings.location: Bilateral periventricular and subcortical white matter

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Multifocal confluent periventricular hyperintensities and punctate subcortical white matter hyperintensities
signal_characteristics.DWI: No restricted diffusion in these regions, confirming chronicity

additional_observations: Fazekas grade 2 white matter disease, moderate for age
MRA of the circle of Willis: left MCA is patent with no M1 occlusion; mild irregularity of the left M2 superior division branch suggesting distal stenosis or resolved embolus
Bilateral ACA, right MCA, and bilateral PCA are patent
Mild generalized cerebral volume loss, slightly advanced for age
mri.impression: 1. Acute left MCA superior division infarction involving the left posterior frontal and parietal cortex with restricted diffusion and FLAIR hyperintensity consistent with subacute timing (>4-6 hours). 2. No large vessel occlusion on MRA; mild left M2 superior division irregularity suggesting prior embolic event with partial recanalization. 3. Moderate chronic small vessel ischemic disease (Fazekas grade 2). 4. No hemorrhagic transformation. The stroke mechanism remains to be determined — further evaluation for cardioembolic versus large artery atherosclerotic etiology is recommended.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Acute ischemic infarction, left MCA superior division territory
differential_by_imaging.key_features: DWI restriction with ADC confirmation in a vascular territory distribution with FLAIR positivity indicating established infarct
No evidence of intraparenchymal hemorrhage on any sequence
Vascular territory distribution and clinical context strongly favor ischemic etiology over demyelination

recommended_actions: CT angiography or MRA of the neck for cervical vessel evaluation
Cardiac monitoring for paroxysmal atrial fibrillation
Follow-up imaging in 24-48 hours to assess for hemorrhagic transformation

findings: Left atrial enlargement (P-wave duration >120 ms in lead II, biphasic P-wave in V1 with terminal negative component >1 mm and >40 ms)
Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria (SV1 + RV5 = 38 mm)
Nonspecific ST-T wave changes in leads V5-V6
No acute ST elevation or depression
ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm at 78 bpm. Left atrial enlargement. Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria with secondary repolarization changes. No acute ischemic changes. No arrhythmia detected on this tracing.
ecg.clinical_correlation: Left atrial enlargement is a risk factor for atrial fibrillation and may indicate underlying paroxysmal AF not captured on this single tracing. Prolonged cardiac monitoring is recommended. LVH is consistent with chronic hypertension.

CBC.clinical_significance: Mildly low, may reflect chronic disease or nutritional deficiency

BMP.clinical_significance: Elevated, consistent with known chronic kidney disease
Elevated, consistent with CKD stage 3a (eGFR approximately 52)
Elevated, consistent with known diabetes and possible stress hyperglycemia

Lipid Panel.clinical_significance: Elevated despite statin therapy, suboptimal control
Well above target for secondary prevention in a patient with prior TIA
Low HDL, additional cardiovascular risk factor
Significantly elevated, associated with metabolic syndrome and insulin resistance

Cardiac and Other.clinical_significance: Poorly controlled diabetes, significant modifiable stroke risk factor
Mildly elevated, may suggest subclinical heart failure or left atrial strain; correlates with left atrial enlargement on ECG

labs.interpretation: Laboratory results demonstrate findings consistent with multiple poorly controlled vascular risk factors: elevated creatinine and BUN reflecting CKD stage 3a, hyperglycemia with HbA1c 8.4% indicating poorly controlled diabetes, suboptimal lipid panel despite statin therapy, mildly low hemoglobin, and mildly elevated BNP suggesting possible cardiac strain. Coagulation studies are normal. Troponin is within normal limits, excluding concurrent acute myocardial injury.
abnormal_values_summary: Hemoglobin: 12.4 g/dL - Mildly low, may reflect chronic disease or nutritional deficiency
BUN: 30 mg/dL - Elevated, consistent with known chronic kidney disease
Creatinine: 1.4 mg/dL - Elevated, consistent with CKD stage 3a (eGFR approximately 52)
Glucose: 154 mg/dL - Elevated, consistent with known diabetes and possible stress hyperglycemia
Total Cholesterol: 222 mg/dL - Elevated despite statin therapy, suboptimal control
LDL: 121 mg/dL - Well above target for secondary prevention in a patient with prior TIA
HDL: 35 mg/dL - Low HDL, additional cardiovascular risk factor
Triglycerides: 226 mg/dL - Significantly elevated, associated with metabolic syndrome and insulin resistance
HbA1c: 8.1 % - Poorly controlled diabetes, significant modifiable stroke risk factor
BNP: 188 pg/mL - Mildly elevated, may suggest subclinical heart failure or left atrial strain; correlates with left atrial enlargement on ECG

findings.location: Left internal carotid artery at the bifurcation

signal_characteristics.CTA: Mixed calcified and soft plaque at the left carotid bifurcation with approximately 60-70% stenosis (NASCET criteria). Irregular plaque surface suggesting possible ulceration.

findings.location: Right internal carotid artery at the bifurcation

signal_characteristics.CTA: Calcified plaque at the right carotid bifurcation with approximately 30% stenosis (NASCET criteria). Smooth plaque surface.
Focal moderate stenosis of the left MCA M2 superior division, estimated 40-50%. No complete occlusion. Distal branches are opacified.

additional_observations: Bilateral vertebral arteries are patent with normal caliber
Basilar artery is patent without significant stenosis
Right MCA is patent without stenosis
No evidence of cervical vessel dissection
Aortic arch demonstrates mild atherosclerotic calcification, type II arch
output.impression: 1. Moderate-to-severe (60-70%) left ICA stenosis with irregular plaque morphology, raising concern for artery-to-artery embolism as the stroke mechanism. 2. Focal moderate stenosis of the left MCA M2 superior division, which may represent intracranial atherosclerosis or residual thrombus from an embolic event. 3. Mild right ICA stenosis. 4. No cervical vessel dissection. The combination of extracranial and intracranial left-sided atherosclerotic disease is consistent with a large artery atherosclerotic etiology, though cardioembolic source should still be excluded.
recommended_actions: Consider carotid endarterectomy or stenting consultation for symptomatic 60-70% left ICA stenosis
Prolonged cardiac monitoring to exclude paroxysmal atrial fibrillation
Optimization of medical therapy: high-intensity statin, dual antiplatelet consideration
Repeat CTA or MRA in 3-6 months to reassess intracranial stenosis

Transthoracic Echocardiogram.clinical_significance: Mildly dilated left atrium, consistent with left atrial enlargement on ECG and raising suspicion for paroxysmal atrial fibrillation
Likely functional, related to LVH and atrial dilation

Transthoracic Echocardiogram.value: No patent foramen ovale or atrial septal defect identified on color Doppler; bubble study not performed

output.interpretation: Transthoracic echocardiogram reveals low-normal LVEF (55%), concentric left ventricular hypertrophy consistent with chronic hypertension, mildly dilated left atrium (LAVI 36 mL/m2), grade I diastolic dysfunction, and mild mitral regurgitation. No intracardiac thrombus identified. The dilated left atrium in combination with ECG findings of left atrial enlargement raises concern for paroxysmal atrial fibrillation as a potential contributor to the stroke mechanism. Bubble study or transesophageal echocardiogram may be considered if cryptogenic source is suspected.
abnormal_values_summary: Left Atrial Volume Index: 36 mL/m2 - Mildly dilated left atrium, consistent with left atrial enlargement on ECG and raising suspicion for paroxysmal atrial fibrillation
Left Ventricular Wall Thickness: Concentric LVH (septal thickness 1.3 cm)  - Consistent with longstanding hypertension
Diastolic Function: Grade I diastolic dysfunction (impaired relaxation)  - Consistent with hypertensive heart disease
Mitral Valve: Mild mitral regurgitation  - Likely functional, related to LVH and atrial dilation
Aortic Valve: Mild aortic sclerosis without stenosis  - Degenerative change, not hemodynamically significant

output.rhythm: Predominantly normal sinus rhythm with paroxysmal atrial fibrillation
findings: Predominantly normal sinus rhythm (approximately 92% of recording)

Three episodes of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation totaling 1 hour 52 minutes (8% of recording): Episode 1 at 02:14, duration 38 minutes, max rate 124 bpm; Episode 2 at 04:47, duration 52 minutes, max rate 132 bpm; Episode 3 at 15:22, duration 22 minutes, max rate 118 bpm
Frequent premature atrial complexes (PACs), 847 in 24 hours (approximately 1% burden)
Average heart rate 74 bpm during sinus rhythm (range 52-96 bpm)
Rare premature ventricular complexes (18 in 24 hours)
No sustained or non-sustained ventricular tachycardia
output.interpretation: Twenty-four hour Holter monitoring reveals predominantly normal sinus rhythm with three episodes of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation totaling approximately 1 hour 52 minutes. The AF burden is approximately 8% with maximum ventricular rates up to 132 bpm during AF episodes. Frequent premature atrial complexes are present. No significant ventricular arrhythmias or pauses.
output.clinical_correlation: The discovery of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation in this patient with recent ischemic stroke fundamentally changes the stroke mechanism classification from presumed large artery atherosclerotic to potentially cardioembolic or mixed mechanism. This finding has major therapeutic implications: anticoagulation is now indicated for secondary stroke prevention, as antiplatelet therapy alone is insufficient for AF-related stroke prevention. The CHA2DS2-VASc score is elevated (hypertension, age 65-74, diabetes, prior stroke/TIA, vascular disease = minimum 5 points), conferring high annual stroke risk without anticoagulation.

Hypercoagulability Panel.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated homocysteine, a modifiable vascular risk factor; consider supplementation with folate and B12

output.interpretation: Hypercoagulability panel is essentially normal. No hereditary or acquired thrombophilia identified. Mildly elevated homocysteine at 16.8 umol/L is a modifiable risk factor that can be addressed with folate and vitamin B12 supplementation. The stroke etiology is most consistent with large artery atherosclerotic disease and/or paroxysmal atrial fibrillation (if identified on prolonged monitoring) rather than a hypercoagulable state.
abnormal_values_summary: Homocysteine: 16.0 umol/L - Mildly elevated homocysteine, a modifiable vascular risk factor; consider supplementation with folate and B12

findings.location: Left internal carotid artery at the bifurcation

signal_characteristics.Plaque_morphology: Heterogeneous, predominantly hypoechoic plaque with irregular surface — features associated with higher embolic risk

findings.location: Right internal carotid artery at the bifurcation

signal_characteristics.Plaque_morphology: Predominantly calcified plaque with smooth surface

additional_observations: Bilateral common carotid arteries demonstrate mildly increased intima-media thickness
Bilateral vertebral arteries are patent with antegrade flow
No evidence of vertebral artery stenosis
output.impression: 1. Moderate (60-69%) stenosis of the left internal carotid artery with heterogeneous, predominantly hypoechoic plaque and irregular surface morphology — features associated with higher embolic risk. This is a symptomatic stenosis ipsilateral to the acute infarct. 2. Mild (<50%) stenosis of the right internal carotid artery with stable calcified plaque. 3. Patent bilateral vertebral arteries. Vascular surgery or neurointerventional consultation is recommended for consideration of carotid endarterectomy or stenting given symptomatic moderate stenosis with high-risk plaque features.
recommended_actions: Vascular surgery consultation for symptomatic left ICA stenosis
Consider carotid endarterectomy given symptomatic 60-69% stenosis with high-risk plaque
Optimize medical therapy: high-intensity statin, antihypertensives, diabetes management
Repeat carotid duplex in 6 months for the right side

output.query: wake-up stroke thrombolysis tissue clock DWI-FLAIR mismatch management guidelines

results.title: MRI-Guided Thrombolysis for Stroke with Unknown Time of Onset (WAKE-UP Trial)
results.authors: Thomalla G, Simonsen CZ, et al.
results.key_finding: IV alteplase guided by DWI-FLAIR mismatch (DWI positive, FLAIR negative) resulted in better functional outcomes compared to placebo in wake-up stroke patients. DWI-FLAIR mismatch suggests stroke onset within approximately 4.5 hours.

results.title: Extending the Time for Thrombolysis in Emergency Neurological Deficits (EXTEND Trial)
results.authors: Ma H, Campbell BCV, et al.
results.key_finding: IV alteplase 4.5-9 hours after onset (or wake-up stroke) improved outcomes when guided by perfusion imaging showing salvageable penumbra.

results.title: 2019 AHA/ASA Guideline Update: Early Management of Acute Ischemic Stroke
results.authors: Powers WJ, Rabinstein AA, et al.
results.key_finding: For wake-up strokes, IV alteplase may be considered if DWI-FLAIR mismatch is present, suggesting stroke occurred within 4.5 hours. If FLAIR is already positive, patient is likely outside the safe thrombolysis window.

output.summary: In wake-up stroke, the DWI-FLAIR mismatch concept serves as a 'tissue clock' to identify patients who may still benefit from IV thrombolysis despite unknown onset time. A DWI-positive/FLAIR-negative pattern suggests onset within approximately 4.5 hours and may support thrombolysis eligibility (WAKE-UP trial). However, when FLAIR is already positive (as in this case), the infarct is likely older than 4.5 hours, and IV thrombolysis carries greater risk without clear benefit. CT perfusion-guided approaches (EXTEND trial) offer an alternative pathway for late-window patients. For patients with LVO, thrombectomy remains an option up to 24 hours based on perfusion imaging (DAWN/DEFUSE-3 criteria), but this patient does not have a proximal LVO.

interactions: Aspirin 81 mg: Increased bleeding risk with concomitant anticoagulant and antiplatelet therapy. Generally, antiplatelet should be discontinued when anticoagulation is initiated for AF-related stroke prevention, unless there is a concomitant indication such as recent coronary stenting.
contraindications: CKD stage 3a (eGFR 52): Apixaban does not require dose adjustment at this level of renal function. Standard dosing (5 mg BID) is appropriate. Dose reduction to 2.5 mg BID is indicated if two of three criteria are met: age >=80, weight <=60 kg, or creatinine >=1.5 mg/dL. This patient meets only one criterion (creatinine 1.52), so standard dose is used.
warnings: Monitor renal function periodically as CKD may progress, potentially requiring dose adjustment
Avoid concomitant use of NSAIDs due to increased bleeding risk
Patient should be counseled regarding signs of bleeding and advised to carry anticoagulation identification
output.formulary_status: Preferred formulary agent for non-valvular atrial fibrillation
alternatives: Rivaroxaban 20 mg daily (requires dose reduction with CrCl 15-50; current eGFR 52 allows full dose)
Edoxaban 60 mg daily (requires dose reduction if CrCl 15-50)
Warfarin (target INR 2-3; requires frequent monitoring, more drug-food interactions)

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke, left MCA superior division territory, with likely mixed etiology (large artery atherosclerotic disease and newly discovered paroxysmal atrial fibrillation)

differential.key_distinguishing: MRI shows no hemorrhage; DWI restriction in a vascular territory confirms ischemic etiology
No seizure history, no witnessed seizure, deficits persistent over hours, and MRI confirms acute infarction
Age 67 without migraine history; persistent deficits with confirmatory DWI restriction on MRI rules this out
ED glucose 162 mg/dL; hyperglycemia rather than hypoglycemia is present
MRI shows vascular territory DWI restriction without enhancing mass; acute presentation inconsistent with neoplasm

optimal_actions.action: Activate stroke code, establish last known well time (22:30 prior evening), perform NIHSS assessment, and obtain fingerstick glucose
optimal_actions.expected_finding: NIHSS 5, LKW >9 hours prior, glucose elevated

optimal_actions.action: Obtain emergent non-contrast CT head to exclude hemorrhage
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No hemorrhage, possible subtle early ischemic changes

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI with DWI and FLAIR to evaluate for acute infarction and assess DWI-FLAIR mismatch for possible thrombolysis eligibility
optimal_actions.expected_finding: DWI restriction with FLAIR positivity in left MCA superior division territory, indicating infarct older than 4.5 hours — not eligible for IV thrombolysis based on tissue clock

optimal_actions.action: Obtain stat labs including CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, lipid panel, HbA1c, and troponin
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated creatinine, hyperglycemia, poorly controlled HbA1c, suboptimal lipids, normal coagulation
Sinus rhythm with left atrial enlargement and LVH — no AF on initial tracing but LAE raises suspicion

optimal_actions.action: Obtain CTA head and neck for vascular evaluation given suspected large artery etiology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Moderate left ICA stenosis 60-70% with irregular plaque, focal left M2 stenosis

optimal_actions.action: Initiate continuous telemetry monitoring and order prolonged cardiac monitoring (Holter or event monitor) to detect paroxysmal atrial fibrillation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Paroxysmal AF episodes detected, fundamentally changing stroke mechanism classification
LVH, dilated left atrium, diastolic dysfunction — supportive of cardioembolic risk

optimal_actions.action: Admit to stroke unit for monitoring, initiate aspirin 325 mg in acute phase, and manage blood pressure with permissive hypertension (avoid aggressive lowering)

After paroxysmal AF is identified, transition from antiplatelet to anticoagulation therapy (apixaban 5 mg BID) for secondary stroke prevention, typically initiated 4-14 days post-stroke depending on infarct size
Consult vascular surgery for consideration of carotid endarterectomy given symptomatic 60-69% left ICA stenosis with high-risk plaque features
Optimize vascular risk factor management: intensify statin therapy (switch to high-intensity), optimize antihypertensives, improve diabetic control, smoking cessation counseling

critical_actions: Establish last known well time to determine thrombolysis eligibility — recognize patient is outside standard IV tPA window
Evaluate DWI-FLAIR mismatch on MRI to determine if tissue-clock-based thrombolysis is appropriate (it is not, given FLAIR positivity)
Obtain vascular imaging (CTA or MRA) to identify large artery atherosclerotic disease as a potential stroke mechanism
Initiate prolonged cardiac monitoring to detect paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, despite normal sinus rhythm on initial ECG
Recognize left atrial enlargement on ECG as a clue to underlying paroxysmal AF
Transition to anticoagulation once paroxysmal AF is identified
Recognize the right-sided neglect on examination as evidence of a left parietal stroke
contraindicated_actions: Administering IV alteplase without confirming DWI-FLAIR mismatch in a wake-up stroke — FLAIR is already positive in this case, indicating the infarct is outside the safe thrombolysis window
Aggressively lowering blood pressure below 140/90 in the acute phase (may worsen cerebral perfusion to penumbral tissue)
Failing to pursue prolonged cardiac monitoring and assuming stroke is purely atherosclerotic based on normal sinus rhythm on initial ECG
Discharging patient on aspirin alone without investigating for paroxysmal atrial fibrillation
Performing carotid endarterectomy before adequate medical stabilization and cardiac workup
key_reasoning_points: Wake-up stroke with unknown exact onset time requires tissue-based imaging (DWI-FLAIR mismatch) rather than time-based criteria to determine thrombolysis eligibility
FLAIR positivity in this case indicates the infarct is older than 4.5 hours, making IV thrombolysis inadvisable despite the desire to treat
Normal sinus rhythm on initial ECG does not exclude paroxysmal atrial fibrillation — left atrial enlargement on ECG and dilated LA on echo are red flags requiring prolonged monitoring

The discovery of paroxysmal AF on Holter monitoring fundamentally changes the stroke mechanism from presumed large artery atherosclerotic to cardioembolic or mixed, with major therapeutic implications (anticoagulation vs. antiplatelet alone)
The combination of symptomatic left ICA stenosis and newly discovered paroxysmal AF creates a mixed-mechanism scenario requiring both anticoagulation and vascular surgical consultation
Subtle right-sided neglect (line bisection deviation, visual extinction) localizes the lesion to the left parietal lobe, consistent with the MRI findings in the left MCA superior division territory

Multiple poorly controlled modifiable risk factors (hypertension, diabetes, hyperlipidemia, smoking, OSA non-adherence) contribute to the overall vascular burden and must be addressed for secondary prevention

metadata.difficulty_description: Moderate - wake-up stroke with subtle right-sided neglect and mild dysarthria, normal sinus rhythm on initial ECG masking paroxysmal AF, vascular risk factors present with mixed-mechanism etiology requiring comprehensive workup
clinical_pearls: Left atrial enlargement on ECG in a stroke patient should prompt aggressive screening for paroxysmal atrial fibrillation with prolonged cardiac monitoring
DWI-FLAIR mismatch serves as a tissue clock for wake-up strokes: DWI+/FLAIR- suggests onset within 4.5 hours and potential thrombolysis eligibility; DWI+/FLAIR+ suggests the window has passed
Up to 25% of ischemic strokes initially classified as cryptogenic are later found to have paroxysmal AF on prolonged monitoring
Symptomatic carotid stenosis 50-69% may benefit from endarterectomy, especially with high-risk plaque features (irregular surface, hypoechoic composition)
